The Cost of Premature Abstraction

Abstractions feel productive. They feel like progress. But every abstraction is also a bet on the future shape of the problem — and most of those bets are wrong.

The three-instance rule

Wait until you have three concrete instances of the same pattern before reaching for an abstraction. With two instances, your generalization is almost certainly wrong.

Inlined repetition is cheap

Three nearly-identical lines of code are easier to read, change, and delete than one cleverly parameterized helper. The helper hides the differences; the lines surface them.

Related: Five rules for working with legacy code.
